title: Third Blog Post
text:
The source for any page in your website could also be a Jupyter Notebook. This one is third-post/index.ipynb.
Here’s an example I borrowed from the Seaborn docs:

import seaborn as sns

sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid")

# Load the diamonds dataset
diamonds = sns.load_dataset("diamonds")

# Plot the distribution of clarity ratings, conditional on carat
sns.displot(
    data=diamonds,
    x="carat", hue="cut",
    kind="kde", height=4, aspect=1.5,
    multiple="fill", clip=(0, None),
    palette="ch:rot=-.25,hue=1,light=.75",   
)

title: Projects
text:
Predicting House Prices with Machine Learning



Python

Machine Learning

Data Cleaning



This project involves using machine learning algorithms to predict house prices based on various features such as location, size, and amenities. It includes data cleaning, feature engineering, and model selection.



Jan 1, 2024













Customer Segmentation Using Clustering Techniques



R

Machine Learning

Clustering

Statistical Modelling



This project focuses on segmenting customers into different groups based on their purchasing behavior and demographics. It uses clustering algorithms like K-means and hierarchical clustering to identify distinct customer segments.



Apr 1, 2024













Visualizing Global CO2 Emissions



R

Data Visualization

Environmental Science



This project involves creating visualizations to show trends in global CO2 emissions over time. It includes data extraction from public databases, data cleaning, and using visualization libraries to create interactive charts and graphs.



Jul 1, 2024






No matching items

title: Alleahrose Jaroma
text: A little bit about me and my life. I have lived in five different states, Rhode Island, Georgia, South Carolina, Tennessee, and Florida. I love to travel and read books. I volunteer at VOCAL Voices of Change Animal League.

title: Homework 3
text:
Objective: This assignment aims to enhance your skills in data manipulation, specifically in reshaping data between long and wide formats, and in creating effective visualizations using ggplot2. By completing this exercise, you will learn to prepare data to answer specific questions and use appropriate graphs to communicate your findings.
Dataset: We will be working with a dataset containing information about insurance company assets stored in “naic_asset.rta”. This dataset includes the following variables: COCODE (company code), COMPANY_NAME, COMPANY_TYPE_DESC (company type), CURRENT_YEAR (asset value), YEAR (reporting year), and LINE (line of business). The LINE variable includes “Bonds”, “CashHandDeposit”, “Stocks_Com”, and “Total”.
Data Preparation and Reshaping:

Data Loading and Filtering:

Load the naic_asset.rta dataset.
Select the following columns: COCODE, COMPANY_NAME, COMPANY_TYPE_DESC, zCURRENT_YEAR, YEAR, and LINE.
Filter the data to retain only the rows where the LINE variable is either “Bonds”, “CashHandDeposit”, “Stocks_Com”, or “Total”.
Store the resulting data frame as longdata.



rm(list = ls())
library(tidyverse)

── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.6
✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
✔ ggplot2   4.0.1     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.5     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
✔ purrr     1.2.1     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

load("naic_asset.rta")
longdata <- MyData |> select(COCODE, COMPANY_NAME, COMPANY_TYPE_DESC, CURRENT_YEAR, YEAR, LINE) |> filter(LINE %in% c("Bonds", "CashHandDeposit", "Stocks_Com", "Total"))


Pivoting to Wide Format:

Transform the longdata from its current long format to a wide format.
Use the unique values in the LINE column (“Bonds”, “CashHandDeposit”, “Stocks_Com”, “Total”) as the names of the new columns (i.e., “Bonds”, “CashHandDeposit”, “Stocks_Com”, and “Total”).
Populate these new columns with the values from the CURRENT_YEAR column.
Retain all the other columns COCODE, COMPANY_NAME, COMPANY_TYPE_DESC, and YEAR.
Make sure all observations have a positive (non-zero) total assets (Total).
Store the resulting data frame as widedata.



widedata <- longdata |> pivot_wider(names_from = LINE, values_from = CURRENT_YEAR) |> filter(!is.na(Total) & Total > 0)

widedata

# A tibble: 38,305 × 8
   COCODE COMPANY_NAME             COMPANY_TYPE_DESC  YEAR      Bonds Stocks_Com
    <dbl> <chr>                    <chr>             <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl>
 1  60003 Park Avenue Life Ins Co  STOCK              1989    4.94e 7    9889686
 2  60011 Abraham Lincoln Ins Co   STOCK              1989    4.89e 6        105
 3  60020 Gulf Natl Life Ins Co    STOCK              1989    1.96e 6    1625417
 4  60038 Acacia Life Ins Co       STOCK              1989    4.88e 8   87821247
 5  60046 Academy Life Ins Co      STOCK              1989    1.72e 8    6394088
 6  60054 Aetna Life Ins Co        STOCK              1989    1.50e10  191259530
 7  60097 All American Life Ins Co STOCK              1989    6.03e 8     358736
 8  60135 Alliance Life Ins Co     STOCK              1989    5.78e 6     225500
 9  60178 Allied Life Ins Co       STOCK              1989    9.81e 7      30151
10  60186 Allstate Life Ins Co     STOCK              1989    7.35e 9  398910385
# ℹ 38,295 more rows
# ℹ 2 more variables: CashHandDeposit <dbl>, Total <dbl>



Creating Ratio Variables and Converting Data Types:

Within widedata, calculate two new variables:

bondRatio: Compute this by dividing the values in the Bonds column by the corresponding values in the Total column.
stockRatio: Compute this by dividing the values in the Stocks_Com column by the corresponding values in the Total column.

Convert both the YEAR and COMPANY_TYPE_DESC columns into factors.



widedata <- widedata |> mutate(bondRatio = Bonds / Total, stockRatio = Stocks_Com / Total, YEAR = as.factor(YEAR), COMPANY_TYPE_DESC = as.factor(COMPANY_TYPE_DESC))

glimpse(widedata)

Rows: 38,305
Columns: 10
$ COCODE            <dbl> 60003, 60011, 60020, 60038, 60046, 60054, 60097, 601…
$ COMPANY_NAME      <chr> "Park Avenue Life Ins Co", "Abraham Lincoln Ins Co",…
$ COMPANY_TYPE_DESC <fct> STOCK, STOCK, STOCK, STOCK, STOCK, STOCK, STOCK, STO…
$ YEAR              <fct> 1989, 1989, 1989, 1989, 1989, 1989, 1989, 1989, 1989…
$ Bonds             <dbl> 49381892, 4894938, 1960848, 488296098, 171596618, 15…
$ Stocks_Com        <dbl> 9889686, 105, 1625417, 87821247, 6394088, 191259530,…
$ CashHandDeposit   <dbl> 50520, 38953, 2249158, 881850, 5785955, 51513066, 29…
$ Total             <dbl> 66601699, 9901392, 12597489, 815113948, 259956156, 5…
$ bondRatio         <dbl> 0.7414509, 0.4943687, 0.1556539, 0.5990526, 0.660098…
$ stockRatio        <dbl> 1.484900e-01, 1.060457e-05, 1.290271e-01, 1.077411e-…



Pivoting back to Long Format:

Transform the widedata back to a long format.
Combine all columns except COCODE, YEAR, COMPANY_NAME, and COMPANY_TYPE_DESC into two columns: a name column and a value column.
The name column should contain the original column names that were transformed (Bonds, CashHandDeposit, Stocks_Com, Total, bondRatio, stockRatio), and the value column should contain the corresponding values.
Store this reshaped data as longdata.



longdata <- widedata |> pivot_longer(cols = -c(COCODE, YEAR, COMPANY_NAME, COMPANY_TYPE_DESC), names_to = "name", values_to = "value")

unique(longdata$name)

[1] "Bonds"           "Stocks_Com"      "CashHandDeposit" "Total"          
[5] "bondRatio"       "stockRatio"     


Data Visualization:

Visualizing Total Asset Trends Over Time:

Create a point chart visualizing the trend of total assets (value) over the years (YEAR).
Filter the longdata to include only the rows where name is equal to “Total”.
Tilt the x-axis labels by 45 degrees for better readability.



longdata |> filter(name == "Total") |> ggplot(aes(x = YEAR, y = value)) + geom_point() + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))









Comparing Total Asset Trends by Company Type:

Enhance the point chart from the previous step by adding color to differentiate the trend of total assets across different company types (COMPANY_TYPE_DESC).
Keep the filter for total assets (name equal to “Total”).
Tilt the x-axis labels by 45 degrees and move the legend to the bottom of the plot for better readability.



longdata |> filter(name == "Total") |> ggplot(aes(x = YEAR, y = value, color = COMPANY_TYPE_DESC)) + geom_point() + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1), legend.position = "bottom")









Analyzing Total Asset Distribution Over Time:

Create a box plot to visualize the distribution of total assets (value) for each year (YEAR).
Filter the longdata to include only the rows where name is equal to “Total”.
Tilt the x-axis labels by 45 degrees.



longdata |> filter(name == "Total") |> ggplot(aes(x = YEAR, y = value)) + geom_boxplot() + scale_y_log10() + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))









Focusing on the Total Asset Distribution:

Recreate the box plot from the previous step but this time add a ylim() function to zoom into the distribution, setting the y-axis limits to range from 0 to 108.



longdata |> filter(name == "Total") |> ggplot(aes(x = YEAR, y = value)) + geom_boxplot() + ylim(0, 1e8) + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

Warning: Removed 16708 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).










Analyzing Bond Ratio Distribution by Company Type:

Create a box plot to show the distribution of the bondRatio for each year (YEAR), coloring the boxes based on COMPANY_TYPE_DESC.
Filter longdata to include only rows where the name variable is “bondRatio”.
Set the y-axis limits from 0 to 1, and tilt x-axis labels by 45 degrees.
Move the legend to the bottom of the plot for better readability.



longdata |> filter(name == "bondRatio") |> ggplot(aes(x = YEAR, y = value, fill = COMPANY_TYPE_DESC)) + geom_boxplot() + ylim(0, 1) + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1), legend.position = "bottom")

Warning: Removed 16 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).










Comparing Bond and Stock Ratio Distributions by Company Type:

Create a faceted box plot to compare the distribution of bondRatio and stockRatio over the years, across different company types.
Filter longdata to include only rows where the name variable is either “bondRatio” or “stockRatio”.
Set the y-axis limits from 0 to 1. Tilt x-axis labels by 45 degrees.
Facet the plot by both the name column (to distinguish between bond ratio and stock ratio) and the COMPANY_TYPE_DESC column.
Move the legend to the bottom of the plot for better readability.



longdata |> filter(name %in% c("bondRatio", "stockRatio")) |> ggplot(aes(x = YEAR, y = value, fill = COMPANY_TYPE_DESC)) + geom_boxplot() + ylim(0, 1) + facet_grid(name ~ COMPANY_TYPE_DESC) + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1), legend.position = "bottom")

Warning: Removed 31 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).










Visualizing Average Bond and Stock Ratios with Stacked Bars:

Filter longdata to include only “bondRatio” and “stockRatio”.
Transform the data into a wide format with bondRatio and stockRatio as separate columns
Group the data by YEAR and COMPANY_TYPE_DESC and calculate the mean of bondRatio and stockRatio for each group.
Transform the data back to long format, with the mean bondRatio and stockRatio values in a single value column, and the type of ratio in a name column.
Create a stacked bar chart showing the average bondRatio and stockRatio over time for each company type.
Use YEAR as the x-axis and the value as the y-axis.
Color the bars based on the name (bond ratio vs. stock ratio).
Facet the plot by COMPANY_TYPE_DESC.
Tilt x-axis labels by 45 degrees and position the legend at the bottom.
Set y-axis limits from 0 to 1.
Add an appropriate title and labels for the x and y axis.



avg_data <- longdata |> filter(name %in% c("bondRatio", "stockRatio")) |> pivot_wider(names_from = name, values_from = value) |> group_by(YEAR, COMPANY_TYPE_DESC) |> summarise(bondRatio  = mean(bondRatio, na.rm = TRUE), stockRatio = mean(stockRatio, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |> pivot_longer(cols = c(bondRatio, stockRatio), names_to = "name", values_to = "value")

ggplot(avg_data, aes(x = YEAR, y = value, fill = name)) + geom_bar(stat = "identity") + facet_wrap(~ COMPANY_TYPE_DESC) + ylim(0, 1) + labs(title = "Average Bond and Stock Ratios Over Time by Company Type", x = "Year", y = "Average Portfolio Ratio", fill = "Investment Type") + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1), legend.position = "bottom")









Briefly describe what you found going through these steps to visualize the data. Going through this assignment working on changing the shape of data and making graphs with ggplot2. The graphs show that total assets are different for each company, grow over time, and that companies put more money in bonds than stocks. Using ggplot2 made it easy to see and understand these patterns.
